subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Mahjong uses 144 tiles, and understanding what they are is the first step to learning the game. The tiles break into three groups: suited tiles, honor tiles, and bonus tiles.

The suited tiles make up the bulk of the set at 108 tiles. There are three suits, each running from rank 1 through rank 9, and every tile exists in four identical copies. The first suit is Bamboo, sometimes called Sticks, showing arrangements of bamboo rods. The second is Characters, also called Wan, which display Chinese numerals. The third is Dots, also called Circles, decorated with round pips. Each suit has 36 tiles, giving you 108 suited tiles in total. Think of them like three parallel number lines from 1 to 9, with four copies of each number.

Next come the honor tiles, which have no rank or sequence. There are two types: Winds and Dragons. The four Wind tiles are East, South, West, and North, with four copies of each for 16 total. The three Dragon tiles are Red Dragon, marked with the character Zhong, Green Dragon marked with Fa, and White Dragon marked with Bai or sometimes shown as a blank face. Four copies of each Dragon gives you 12 Dragon tiles. That is 28 honor tiles altogether.

Finally, there are 8 bonus tiles split into two sets: 4 Flowers numbered 1 through 4, and 4 Seasons also numbered 1 through 4. These do not stay in your hand during play. When you draw one, you set it aside and draw a replacement.

Your set should also include 2 dice for determining the dealer and wall break, scoring sticks or chips for tracking points, and optionally a small wind indicator disc to mark the current prevailing wind direction.

subtopics.title: Building the Wall
subtopics.content:
Before each hand begins, all 144 tiles are shuffled thoroughly face-down on the table, mixing them by hand so no one can identify them. Once the tiles are well mixed, each of the four players builds one section of what is called the wall.

To build your section, gather tiles and arrange them in a straight row, face-down, two tiles high and 18 tiles long. You are essentially stacking pairs of tiles on top of each other across an 18-tile span, giving your section 36 tiles total. If your set excludes the 8 bonus tiles and uses only 136 tiles, each wall section will be 17 tiles long instead.

Once all four players have built their sections, push them together to form a hollow square in the center of the table, with each player's wall segment forming one side. This closed square is the wall, and tiles will be drawn from it throughout the hand. Make sure the corners connect snugly so the wall holds its shape.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Mahjong always seats exactly four players, and each one is assigned a wind direction that matters for scoring. To determine who plays as East, which is also the dealer, one player rolls both dice and the highest roller becomes East. Seating then runs counterclockwise around the table: East, South, West, North.

Once seated, East rolls the two dice again. The total on the dice tells you where to break the wall. Starting from the right end of East's own wall section, count that many stacks inward and separate the wall at that point. The tiles to the right of the break become the dead wall, used only for replacement draws after Kongs and bonus tiles. All regular draws come from the left side of the break, moving counterclockwise around the wall.

Now for the deal. Starting at the break and going counterclockwise, each player takes two stacks of two tiles, which is four tiles at a time. You do this for three rounds, so every player picks up 12 tiles. Then each player takes one more tile, bringing everyone to 13. Finally, East takes one additional tile, giving East a starting hand of 14 while the other three players hold 13. This extra tile is important because East will begin the game by discarding, as we will explain in a moment.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
Before the first discard, every player checks their hand for bonus tiles, meaning any Flowers or Seasons. If you have any, declare them immediately by placing them face-up to the side of your playing area. For each bonus tile you set aside, draw a replacement tile from the back end of the wall, which is the dead wall. If the replacement tile is also a bonus tile, declare it and draw again until you have a full hand of non-bonus tiles.

Once all bonus tiles have been declared and replaced, East begins the hand. Because East holds 14 tiles while a complete turn ends with 13, East simply chooses one tile to discard face-up into the center of the table. This first discard officially starts the hand, and play continues counterclockwise from East, with South drawing next.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Play flows counterclockwise around the table. When it is your turn, you draw one tile from the open end of the wall, adding it to your hand so you temporarily hold 14 tiles. You then choose one tile to discard face-up into the center of the table, bringing your hand back down to 13.

That is the basic rhythm of every turn: draw one, discard one. With each cycle, you are trying to shape your hand toward a winning combination of four melds and one pair, which totals 14 tiles. You want to keep tiles that work together and let go of tiles that do not fit your plan.

What makes Mahjong different from most games is that between turns, any player at the table may interrupt the normal flow by claiming the tile that was just discarded. This creates a constantly shifting dynamic where you are not just building your own hand but also watching what others discard and deciding whether to seize those tiles.

subtopics.title: Melds and Claims
subtopics.content:
A winning Mahjong hand contains exactly four melds and one pair. A meld is a set of three tiles, and there are different types.

A Chow is a sequence of three consecutive tiles in the same suit, such as the 3, 4, and 5 of Dots. You may only call Chow on a tile discarded by the player sitting directly to your left, the one whose turn comes just before yours. Honor tiles cannot form Chows because they have no numerical sequence.

A Pung is a set of three identical tiles, such as three North Wind tiles or three 8 of Bamboo. Unlike a Chow, you can call Pung on any player's discard regardless of where they sit. When you claim a Pung, you reveal the three tiles face-up in front of you, discard a tile, and play resumes counterclockwise from your position.

A Kong is four identical tiles and works differently because it gives you an extra tile. There are three ways to make a Kong. An exposed Kong happens when you hold three matching tiles and someone discards the fourth, so you claim it and reveal all four face-up. A concealed Kong happens when you draw the fourth matching tile yourself from the wall, and you declare it by placing all four tiles face-down so opponents cannot see them. An added Kong happens when you already have an exposed Pung on the table and you draw the fourth tile, adding it to that Pung. After forming any Kong, you must draw a replacement tile from the dead wall before making your discard, because a Kong uses four tiles but only counts as one meld.

When multiple players want the same discarded tile, strict priority applies. A player claiming the tile to complete Mahjong always wins the claim. Kong and Pung claims beat Chow claims. If two players want the same type of claim, the one whose turn would come next in the counterclockwise order gets it.

The pair, sometimes called the eyes, is the final piece of a winning hand. It is simply two identical tiles. You do not reveal the pair until you declare your win.

subtopics.title: Winning and Scoring Patterns
subtopics.content:
When your hand is complete with four melds and one pair, you declare Mahjong to win the hand. There are two ways to complete your hand. Drawing the winning tile yourself from the wall is called Zimo, or self-drawn, and it typically earns a higher score. Claiming another player's discard to complete your hand is called Hu, and the player who discarded that tile is responsible for paying you.

Under MCR, which stands for Mahjong Competition Rules, your hand must be worth a minimum of 8 fan to be a valid win. Fan are scoring points awarded for specific tile patterns and conditions in your hand. If your hand is technically complete but falls short of 8 fan, you cannot declare Mahjong and must keep playing. This minimum prevents players from winning with trivially easy hands and rewards skillful hand-building.

There are dozens of recognized scoring patterns, but here are some you will encounter frequently. All Chows means your four melds are all sequences with no Pungs, a common but low-scoring hand. All Pungs means every meld is a triplet, which scores well. Mixed One Suit uses tiles from only one suit combined with honor tiles. Pure One Suit, called Qingyise, is more difficult, requiring every tile to come from a single suit with no honors at all, and it scores highly. All Honors is a rare hand composed entirely of Wind and Dragon tiles. Thirteen Orphans, known as Shisanyao, requires one of each of the 13 different terminal and honor tiles plus one duplicate, making it one of the most dramatic hands in the game. Seven Pairs breaks the standard structure entirely, consisting of seven distinct pairs instead of four melds and one pair.

Your bonus tiles also contribute to scoring. Each Flower and Season tile is numbered 1 through 4, corresponding to the four seat winds: 1 is East, 2 is South, 3 is West, and 4 is North. If you draw the bonus tile matching your seat wind, you earn extra fan. Collecting all four Flowers or all four Seasons earns a substantial bonus.

Finally, Winds play a scoring role beyond bonus tiles. The game progresses through rounds with a prevailing wind, starting with East and advancing to South, West, and North. If you hold a Pung of the current prevailing wind, you earn bonus fan. A Pung of your own seat wind also earns bonus fan. If your seat wind happens to match the prevailing wind, a single Pung of that wind earns both bonuses together.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
A hand ends in one of two ways. Either a player declares Mahjong with a valid winning hand, or the wall runs out of drawable tiles and the hand ends in a draw, called Huang Zhuang or a dead hand.

When someone wins, they reveal their hand and calculate the total fan earned from all scoring patterns present. The fan total is then converted to points using a scoring table, where higher fan values yield significantly more points. If you won by claiming a discard, the player who discarded the winning tile pays you the full amount. If you won by self-draw, all three opponents share the payment equally. This difference makes discarding a dangerous tile a costly mistake and adds weight to every discard decision.

If the wall is exhausted and no one has declared Mahjong, the hand is a draw. No points change hands, and the deal simply passes to the next player counterclockwise, who becomes the new East for the following hand.

A full game of Chinese Mahjong spans multiple rounds. In each round, the dealership rotates so that every player serves as East at least once. After a complete rotation, the prevailing wind advances from East to South, then West, then North. A standard game of four full rounds can produce 16 or more individual hands and may take several hours to complete.

At the end of the agreed number of rounds, the player with the highest cumulative point total across all hands is the winner.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
When you first pick up your 13 tiles, take a moment to survey what you have. Identify which suit appears most often in your hand, because leaning into your strongest suit gives you the best foundation for completing melds. If you have six Bamboo tiles and only two Characters, build around Bamboo and start releasing those isolated Characters.

Discard isolated honor tiles early in the hand unless you already hold a pair or triplet of them. Since honors cannot form sequences, a single Wind or Dragon sitting alone in your hand has limited potential and is usually safe to let go. However, if you hold two of the same honor, keep them. That pair could become a Pung if you draw or claim the third copy, or it could serve as the pair your winning hand needs.

In these opening turns, focus on building toward a realistic hand shape. It is tempting to aim for a spectacular pattern like Thirteen Orphans or Pure One Suit, but unless your starting tiles strongly support it, you are better off pursuing a straightforward combination of mixed melds. Flexibility in the early game leads to more wins than ambition.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
One of the most frequent errors new players make is calling Chow too early. Every time you claim a discard to form a Chow or Pung, those tiles go face-up in front of you where everyone can see them. This reveals which suit you are collecting and alerts opponents to stop discarding tiles you might need. Beyond the information leak, concealed hands score higher than exposed ones under MCR, so keeping your hand hidden preserves both secrecy and scoring potential.

Another common trap is chasing an impossible hand. If you need a specific tile to complete a high-value pattern but two or three copies of it are already visible in the discard pool, your chances are slim or nonexistent. Good players constantly scan the discards and adjust their plans accordingly. Be willing to abandon an attractive-looking hand for a more achievable one. Flexibility wins more hands than stubbornness.

Defensive play is something beginners often overlook entirely. When another player has multiple exposed melds and appears to be one tile away from winning, continuing to push for your own hand can be reckless. Instead, shift to defense by discarding tiles that have already appeared in the discard pool, since those are confirmed safe. Avoid discarding tiles in the suit your opponent is clearly collecting, and accept that sometimes the smartest play is ensuring nobody wins rather than risking a costly loss.

Finally, never forget the minimum fan requirement. Under MCR, completing four melds and a pair is not enough on its own. Your hand must be worth at least 8 fan. A hand full of simple mixed Chows with a non-scoring pair might look complete, but if it falls short of 8 fan, you cannot declare Mahjong. Always count your fan before calling a win.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
The most important strategic tension in Mahjong is choosing between keeping your hand concealed and exposing melds by claiming discards. A concealed hand preserves maximum flexibility because you can change direction at any time, and it scores higher under MCR since several valuable patterns require a fully concealed hand. The tradeoff is speed. You can only add tiles from the wall on your own turn, which means building a complete hand takes longer. Exposing melds by calling Chow or Pung accelerates your progress but locks those tiles in place, narrows your options, and gives opponents information about your strategy.

The other critical decision is knowing when to shift from offense to defense. If you believe another player is one tile away from completing their hand, called being in tenpai, and your own hand is far from ready, it is usually correct to stop pursuing your own win entirely. Instead, focus on discarding tiles that are safe, meaning tiles that have already been discarded or tiles that the threatening player has already passed on. A round that ends in a draw costs you nothing, but carelessly discarding the tile that completes an opponent's hand means you pay the full penalty alone. Learning to recognize when to abandon your offensive plan is one of the most valuable skills in Mahjong.

metadata.notes: Chinese Mahjong (MCR — Mahjong Competition Rules). This guide does NOT cover American Mahjong, Japanese Riichi, Hong Kong Old Style, Taiwanese, or other regional variants. Content rewritten to production quality. Source: World Mahjong Organisation official rules.
